title: CBA Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of CBA with 25 questions.

questions.question: You're evaluating an edge-first, privacy-preserving image moderation model that runs on-device for user-uploaded photos in a social commerce app. The system flags policy-violating content without uploading raw media. Build an 18-month CBA: target model size, on-device CPU/memory, energy impact, local telemetry/storage, update cadence, regulatory/compliance costs, rollout plan, go/no-go criteria, and break-even horizon?

options.text: On-device image moderation to avoid uploading raw media. 18-month CBA: target model ~5–10 MB quantized, 1–3 inference ms on modern mobile CPU per image, 0.2–0.6J energy, 1–3 MB per user telemetry, 6–1
2 engineers x 3 weeks = about $26k labor; tooling $3k; storage overhead $50/mo. Privacy penalties avoided: ~$20k/yr. BI adoption uplift 8–12%, retention +2%, tickets reduced. Break-even ~9–12 months,

Estimate per-device GPU/CPU cycles, memory, and energy for offline vector rendering; balance with cloud-rendering costs and bandwidth. Include local cache size for charts, offline latency, and privacy

On-device ASR+translation for offline multilingual chat. Target per-language model 60–120 MB; latency <=150 ms; CPU ~8–12%; battery overhead ~1–2%/min; telemetry ~20 KB/user/mo; quarterly federated up

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This explores a fresh edge-on privacy scenario: on-device content moderation with real-world constraints, asking for a practical CBA that includes model size, latency, energy, telemetry, compliance, rollout, and break-even.

## Key Concepts
- On-device ML model sizing and quantization
- Latency and energy budgets on iOS/Android
- Local telemetry vs privacy guarantees
- Regulatory/compliance considerations and regional rollout
- Break-even horizon and go/no-go criteria

## Code Example
```javascript
function breakEven(beCostMonthly, onDeviceCostMonthly) {
  if (onDeviceCostMonthly <= 0) return Infinity;
  return Math.ceil(beCostMonthly / onDeviceCostMonthly);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate false positives and user impact?
- How would you handle updates during low-bandwidth regions or offline modes?

Use a lightweight in‑app inverted index with incremental updates. Estimate storage 2–5 KB per note, indexing energy 5–15 mJ per document, queries under 20 ms on mid-range devices. Privacy: index store

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses familiarity with local-first design, cost modeling, and privacy implications for a user-facing feature that operates offline.

## Key Concepts
- Local-first indexing, incremental/delta indexing, privacy-by-design
- Resource budgeting: storage, CPU energy, latency
- Rollout strategy, consent flows, maintenance

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudocode: deltaIndex(note) returns terms for incremental update
function deltaIndex(note) {
  const terms = tokenize(note.content);
  return { noteId: note.id, terms };
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle deletions or edits to notes after indexing?
- How would you test performance and energy impact across device tiers?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests the ability to translate a feature idea into a structured cost-benefit plan, balancing on-device compute, energy use, and privacy under real-time constraints.

## Key Concepts
- On-device inference budgeting
- Privacy-by-design and consent
- Offline fallback vs cloud augmentation
- Localization latency and drift maintenance

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudocode: rough per-inference energy budget estimator
function estimateEnergy(perInferenceJ, inferencesPerMonth) {
  return perInferenceJ * inferencesPerMonth;
}
``` 

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate energy estimates in field tests?
- What metrics determine go/no-go milestones for rollout?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses ability to balance multi-tenant QoS, hardware costs, and legal/privacy constraints in a realistic model.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-tenant inference, hardware partitioning, hot updates
- SLA-tied revenue impact, tail latency costs
- Privacy/telemetry, drift validation, fault isolation

## Code Example
```javascript
function cba(regretCost, swapCost, partitionOverhead) {
  // placeholder for a simple cost model sketch
  return regretCost + swapCost + partitionOverhead;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate drift targets in production?
- What data would you collect to calibrate your break-even horizon?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to translate a feature idea into a tangible cost-benefit plan, including rough inputs, trade-offs, and payback.

## Key Concepts
- Cost-benefit analysis
- Activation and conversion uplift
- ARPU and payback period
- TCO: dev time, content, hosting, analytics

## Code Example

```javascript
function breakEven(cost, monthlyRevenue) {
  if (monthlyRevenue <= 0) return Infinity;
  return cost / monthlyRevenue;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate the uplift assumptions before full rollout?
- What experiments would you run to de-risk the estimate (A/B tests, cohort analysis)?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests the ability to structure a pragmatic CBA for ML deployment, balancing cost with user value, handling uncertainty, and communicating the result.

## Key Concepts
- Cost and benefits modeling
- Time horizon and discounting
- Scenario analysis (base/best/worst)
- Metrics: NPV, ROI, risk-adjusted EV

## Code Example
```javascript
function npv(cashFlows, rate) {
  return cashFlows.reduce((acc, cf, i) => acc + cf / Math.pow(1 + rate, i+1), 0);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle non-linear cost factors (e.g., burst traffic) in your model?
- How would you present results to non-technical stakeholders?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Assesses ability to design a privacy-preserving, latency-sensitive on-device feature with a complete financial lens. Tests trade-offs between model compression, energy, and user experience in a realistic conferencing product.

## Key Concepts

- On-device ML: ASR + summarization, quantization, NNAPI/CoreML
- Cost modeling: capex/opex, telemetry, storage, update cadence, regulatory penalties
- Metrics: latency, energy per call, WER/accuracy, storage footprint

## Code Example

```javascript
// Pseudo-cost model
function breakeven(cloudCost, onDeviceCost, months) {
  return (cloudCost - onDeviceCost) / months;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you measure latency/accuracy trade-offs in field tests?
- What update strategy ensures security and minimizes user disruption?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests practical rate-limiting in distributed systems, emphasizing per-user fairness, op readiness, and recovery from clock skew.

## Key Concepts

- Token bucket
- Redis Lua scripts for atomic ops
- Burst handling and backpressure
- Observability and testing

## Code Example

```javascript
// Redis Lua script (conceptual)
local bucket = KEYS[1]
local tokens = tonumber(redis.call('GET', bucket) or '0')
local refill = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local cap = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local new_tokens = math.min(cap, tokens + refill)
if new_tokens >= 1 then
  redis.call('SET', bucket, new_tokens - 1)
  return 1
else
  return 0
end
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test for clock skew and drift?
- How would you adapt for multi-tenant fairness?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to translate a UX feature into a measurable business case, including per-device costs, data budgets, and risk management. It also assesses ability to set clear go/no-go criteria and design monitoring for a beginner-friendly feature.

## Key Concepts

- Cost estimation per device and per user
- Telemetry/data budgets with privacy considerations
- Go/No-Go criteria and risk mitigation

## Code Example

```javascript
// Simple break-even helper
function breakEven(uplift, yearlyCost) {
  if (uplift <= 0) return Infinity;
  return yearlyCost / uplift;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you measure uplift and ensure robust A/B testing?
- What privacy controls would you implement for telemetry data?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question probes ability to translate a feature idea into a concrete 12‑month CBA, focusing on audio DSP, latency, energy, privacy, and rollout constraints in real devices.

## Key Concepts
- On-device DSP and energy budgeting
- Latency targets and memory footprint
- Privacy, data handling, and consent prompts
- Rollout strategy, offline fallback, drift monitoring
- Break-even horizon and go/no-go criteria

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo calculation sketch for CBA milestones
const cba = {
  dspMsPerCall: 0.5,
  energyMw: 15,
  cloudUsagePct: 20,
  latencyMs: 12,
  memMb: 25
};
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you validate QoS in a field trial?
- What privacy safeguards would you implement if cloud calibration is enabled?
- How would you handle device heterogeneity (low-end vs high-end)?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to translate a product concept into a rigorous, privacy‑aware cost model with real hardware and organizational constraints. It probes on-device ML feasibility, data minimization, telemetry design, staged rollout, and long‑term maintenance.

## Key Concepts

- On-device ML cost modeling (model footprint, compute, energy)
- Data minimization and privacy/DP costs
- Telemetry design: anonymized, high‑level signals
- Rollout strategy and break-even analysis
- Drift detection and long‑term maintenance

## Code Example

```javascript
function energyPerEval(modelSizeMB, diffsPerSession) {
  const base = 0.12; // J per 1000 diffs
  return modelSizeMB * 0.01 + base * diffsPerSession;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you validate drift and update cadence in production?
- What telemetry would you collect to measure impact without leaking code content?
- How would you handle offline devices and policy enforcement during rollout?
